messages.content: 请你作为英文改写助手，使我提供给你英文符合SCI论文写作要求
当然可以！请您提供需要改写的英文文段，我将帮您改写成适合SCI(Science Citation Index)论文写作要求的英文。记住，SCI论文通常追求简洁、客观和精确的表述，并且要注重逻辑结构、避免重复，并适当使用专业术语。

The second stage consisted of 264 FPGAs, which were placed on front-end boards to receive the detector data via LVDS links. The second stage transmits data, which receive from first stage, to the third stage through optical links up to 6.25 Gbps.

The second tier of the system architecture was composed of 264 field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs), mounted on front-end boards. These FPGAs were tasked with receiving data from the detectors through low-voltage differential signaling (LVDS) connections. Subsequently, this stage was responsible for the relay of data, initially acquired by the first tier, to the third tier via high-speed optical links, which were capable of supporting transfer rates of up to 6.25 gigabits per second (Gbps).
不够好，请按照学术写作要求重写

In the intermediate phase of the processing pipeline, an array comprising 264 field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) was deployed on dedicated front-end boards. These FPGAs served the critical function of interfacing with detector outputs, acquiring data through low-voltage differential signaling (LVDS) connections. Ensuing the data acquisition, this intermediary stage facilitated the forwarding of the dataset to the subsequent tier via optical links engineered for high-throughput data transmission, achieving a maximum bandwidth of 6.25 Gbps.
不够好

The second stage of the data acquisition system incorporated an ensemble of 264 field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) positioned on front-end circuit boards. These FPGAs were responsible for interfacing directly with the detection apparatus, receiving signals via low-voltage differential signaling (LVDS) links. Following signal acquisition, the FPGAs transmitted the collated data to the third stage utilizing optical links designed for high-bandwidth communication, with transfer rates capable of reaching 6.25 gigabits per second (Gbps).

After receiving the data on the second stage, the hit data are unpacked and grouped into a detector-specific record type (Rec1). The data lines from the different ASICs are merged into groups of two for further processing.

Upon reception at the secondary stage of the system, the incidence data undergo a process of unpacking followed by organization into a structured format specific to the detector type, designated as record type Rec1. Subsequently, data streams emanating from disparate application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs) are consolidated into pairs, thereby facilitating the progression to more advanced stages of processing.

As an example for the developed data processing firmware on the FEB, the data path of the SciFi detector is shown in Fig. 3. Two SciFi module boards (SMBs) holding eight MuTRiG ASICs are connected, via a specific detector adapter board (DAB), to an FEB. After receiving the data on the Arria V FPGA, the hit data are unpacked and grouped into a detector-specific record type (Rec1). The data lines from the different ASICs are merged into groups of two for further processing.

Figure 3 illustrates the data processing pathway implemented in the firmware developed for the Front-End Board (FEB) with reference to the Scintillating Fibre (SciFi) detector. Each FEB interfaces with two SciFi Module Boards (SMBs), each equipped with eight MuTRiG application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs), through an intermediary Detector Adapter Board (DAB). Data received by the Arria V FPGA on the FEB are initially unpacked and subsequently organized into a detector-specific dataset, referred to as record type Rec1. This record type encapsulates the characteristics unique to the SciFi detector. In the ensuing step, the data outputs from the various ASICs are collated in pairs, thereby streamlining the dataset for additional levels of data processing.

The second stage transmits data, which receive from first stage, to the third stage through up to two 6.25 Gbps optical links. The third stage consists of 4 switching boards with high performance FPGA, which is time-align the different data streams.

The second stage of the system is responsible for relaying data received from the initial stage to the third stage through one or two optical links, each capable of handling data transmission speeds up to 6.25 gigabits per second (Gbps). The third stage is comprised of four high-performance switching boards, each equipped with a sophisticated field-programmable gate array (FPGA). These FPGAs perform time alignment across the various incoming data streams, ensuring temporal synchronization as a critical part of the data processing pipeline.

The second stage transmits data, which receive from first stage, to the third stage through up to two 6.25 Gbps optical links. The third stage consists of four switchboards with high-performance FPGAs for time-aligning the different data streams.

Data acquired by the first stage are conveyed to the third stage by the second stage via as many as two optical links with a maximum throughput of 6.25 gigabits per second each. The third stage consists of four switchboards, each fitted with a high-performance FPGA designed to execute time alignment of the various data streams, ensuring synchronous processing and integration of the information.
请帮我总结一下文字

The multi-stage data transmission system progresses from the first to the third stage, channeling data through a maximum of two optical links capable of 6.25 Gbps each. Within the third stage, four switchboards equipped with high-performance FPGAs perform critical time-alignment of the numerous data streams to maintain synchronization.
我现在要给你一段英文，我希望你帮我总结
Absolutely, please provide the text you would like me to summarize for you.

The sixteen 10 Gbit/s links from the switching boards are
routed to the Mu3e filter farm, which consists of twelve PCs
in a daisy chain configuration. Each PC is equipped with a
receiving board. Here we use the commercial DE5aNET board
[35] produced by Terasic Inc. (see Fig. 7). It features an Intel
Arria 10 FPGA connected to four quad small-form-factorpluggable (QSFP) quad-optical transceivers and two banks of
DDR41 memory. It connects to the host PC using one 8-lane
generation 3 PCIe interface.
Fig. 8 shows a schematic of the data flow in a single
farm node. The data from the sixteen links are received, time
aligned, and buffered to the DDR4 memory. If the buffers
are full, the data are forwarded to the next PC in the daisy
chain, providing a simple form of load distribution without
the need for a back channel and back pressure. The hits of
the central pixel detector are extracted from the data stream,
and a first geometrical selection is performed based on hits
from the first three detector layers; only hit triplets compatible
with stemming from a track originating in the target region
are processed further. Using look-up memories, the hit coordinates are transformed from the 32 Bit sensor/column/row
scheme to the global detector coordinate system (using three
single-precision floating point numbers). The hits are then sent
to the main memory of the host PC using DMA transfers. From
the main memory, the hit data are then forwarded to a GPU.2

The data transmission infrastructure of the Mu3e filter farm comprises sixteen 10 Gbps links from switching boards that feed into a chain of twelve daisy-chained PCs. Each PC incorporates a DE5aNET board produced by Terasic Inc., featuring an Intel Arria 10 FPGA, four QSFP optical transceivers, and DDR4 memory, connecting via an 8-lane PCIe Gen 3 interface.

Data flow within each farm node begins with the reception of data from the sixteen links. This data is time-aligned and temporarily stored in DDR4 memory. When memory buffers reach capacity, data is passed to the next PC, offering a straightforward load distribution mechanism without needing a back channel. Initial data processing involves extracting hits from the central pixel detector and making a geometrical selection based on the first three detector layers to identify hit triplets indicative of tracks originating from the target region. Hit coordinates are converted from the sensor's 32-bit encoding to a global detector coordinate system using look-up memories and singled out using floating-point numbers. These hits are then transferred to the host PC's main memory via DMA transfers and subsequently sent to a GPU for further processing.
